subtopic_detail: Paediatric DKA fluid management at presentation
stem: You are the paediatric registrar receiving a 9-year-old non-Aboriginal girl with a 3-week history of polyuria, polydipsia and weight loss. She has been brought in by her parents after a day of vomiting and increasing drowsiness. She is breathing deeply with a fruity odour to her breath and is moderately dehydrated. There is no clinical evidence of cerebral oedema. Her weight today is 28 kg. The endocrinology consultant has confirmed first-presentation type 1 diabetes complicated by DKA and asks you to write up the initial fluid plan.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 °C, HR 132, RR 32 deep Kussmaul, SpO2 99% room air, BP 102/64 mmHg, GCS 14, capillary refill 3 seconds, weight 28 kg
data_table.Examination: Reduced skin turgor, dry mucous membranes, mild acetone breath, alert and orientated, no focal neurological signs
data_table.Investigations: BSL 28.4 mmol/L, ketones 5.8 mmol/L, venous gas pH 7.10 pCO2 19 mmHg HCO3 6 mmol/L base excess minus 22, Na 132 corrected 137 mmol/L, K 4.5 mmol/L, urea 9.0 mmol/L, creatinine 88 micromol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial fluid plan?

options.text: Bolus 20 mL/kg 0.9% sodium chloride over 15 minutes and immediately commence an intravenous insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hour with hourly neurological observations and electrolyte checks
options.rationale: Rapid 20 mL/kg boluses and immediate insulin (before 1 hour of fluid replacement) increase the risk of paediatric cerebral oedema. (Source: APEG paediatric DKA guideline)

options.text: Give 0.45% sodium chloride with 5% dextrose at maintenance plus 10% deficit replacement over 24 hours and commence intravenous insulin at 0.1 units/kg/hour at the same time as fluids start
options.rationale: Insulin should not start until at least 1 hour of intravenous fluids has been delivered in paediatric DKA. (Source: APEG paediatric DKA guideline)

options.text: Give 10 mL/kg 0.9% sodium chloride over 30 minutes, then commence 0.9% sodium chloride with 40 mmol/L potassium chloride at maintenance plus 5-7% deficit over 48 hours and start insulin at 0.05-0.1 units/kg/hour after 1 hour
options.rationale: Paediatric DKA replacement uses cautious boluses (10 mL/kg) and a 48-hour deficit-plus-maintenance approach with insulin started after 1 hour and at 0.05-0.1 units/kg/hour to minimise cerebral oedema risk. (Source: APEG paediatric DKA guideline)

options.text: Give 20 mL/kg of Hartmann's compound sodium lactate over 60 minutes followed by maintenance fluids without potassium and review for insulin commencement when the venous gas has normalised on subsequent venous blood gas
options.rationale: Potassium replacement starts with the initial maintenance bag once urine output is present; awaiting gas normalisation prolongs ketosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

options.text: Give 8.4% sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg over 30 minutes to correct the acidosis before commencing any insulin infusion and use total body water deficit calculation to dictate replacement rate over 12 hours
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is not recommended in paediatric DKA and a 12-hour rapid correction increases cerebral oedema risk. (Source: APEG paediatric DKA guideline)

explanation.summary: Paediatric DKA management is built around cautious volume restoration and slow correction over 48 hours to reduce the risk of cerebral oedema. Initial boluses are typically 10 mL/kg unless there is shock, with the deficit estimated at 5-7% and replaced together with maintenance over 48 hours. Insulin is started after at least 1 hour of fluid at 0.05-0.1 units/kg/hour. Potassium is added once urine output is established. Bicarbonate is avoided.
key_points: Initial bolus 10 mL/kg 0.9% saline unless shocked
Replace 5-7% deficit plus maintenance over 48 hours
Insulin 0.05-0.1 units/kg/hour starting after 1 hour of fluids
Potassium added with first maintenance bag once urine output is present
Avoid bicarbonate; monitor for cerebral oedema with hourly neuro obs
explanation.pearls: Risk factors for cerebral oedema in paediatric DKA include age under 5 years, new-onset diabetes, severe acidosis (pH under 7.1), bicarbonate therapy and rapid fluid administration over 50 mL/kg in the first 4 hours.

subtopic_detail: Suspected airway foreign body in a toddler
stem: You are the paediatric emergency registrar reviewing an 18-month-old non-Aboriginal boy brought in with sudden onset of coughing and stridor while playing in the lounge room. His grandmother thinks he may have put a peanut in his mouth shortly before the episode. He is now alert, breathing spontaneously and stable but has persistent inspiratory stridor and intermittent coughing. There is no cyanosis and he is maintaining oxygen saturation.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 °C, HR 142, RR 36, SpO2 96% room air, BP 92/58 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Awake, alert and consolable on mother's lap, intermittent strong cough, audible inspiratory stridor at rest, mild subcostal recession, breath sounds slightly reduced on right, no drooling, no tripod posture
data_table.Investigations: No imaging yet performed; bedside cardiorespiratory monitoring in place

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Perform 5 back blows followed by 5 chest thrusts immediately because the persistent inspiratory stridor confirms complete airway obstruction warranting basic life support manoeuvres before any expert airway help arrives
options.rationale: Effective coughing and ongoing stridor indicate partial obstruction; back blows and thrusts risk converting partial to complete obstruction. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 4 Airway)

options.text: Administer nebulised adrenaline 1:1000 5 mL and oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg for presumed viral croup and observe in the short stay unit for 4 hours before any specialist referral or further investigation
options.rationale: Sudden onset while eating and asymmetric breath sounds suggest foreign body, not viral croup, and bronchodilators or steroids do not address aspiration. (Source: RCH CPG: Inhaled foreign body)

options.text: Arrange an inspiratory and expiratory chest radiograph and review again only after the imaging is reported by a paediatric radiologist before deciding whether ENT or theatre referral is required
options.rationale: Normal radiographs do not exclude foreign body aspiration; ENT referral should not be deferred for imaging in an evolving airway presentation. (Source: RCH CPG: Inhaled foreign body)

options.text: Commence direct laryngoscopy in the emergency department to retrieve the suspected peanut promptly under topical anaesthesia rather than transfer the child to the operating theatre with anaesthetic and ENT support
options.rationale: Awake direct laryngoscopy in a stridorous toddler risks complete obstruction; rigid bronchoscopy by ENT in theatre with anaesthetic support is the standard. (Source: RCH CPG: Inhaled foreign body)

options.text: Keep the child calm in the parent's arms with supplemental oxygen as tolerated, fast for theatre, contact ENT and anaesthetics urgently for rigid bronchoscopy and avoid distressing procedures or instrumentation
options.rationale: Partial airway obstruction from a suspected foreign body is managed by minimising distress, keeping the child upright in the parent's lap and arranging urgent rigid bronchoscopy under anaesthetic with ENT. (Source: RCH CPG: Inhaled foreign body)

explanation.summary: Suspected foreign body aspiration in a child with effective cough or stridor at rest is partial airway obstruction and is managed by minimising distress, supplemental oxygen as tolerated and urgent rigid bronchoscopy under anaesthetic. Back blows and chest thrusts are reserved for the child with ineffective cough or unconsciousness. Plain radiographs are insensitive for organic foreign bodies and should not delay ENT referral. Inhaled peanut is the commonest organic foreign body in toddlers.
key_points: Partial airway obstruction is managed by keeping the child calm with parents
Rigid bronchoscopy by ENT in theatre is the standard intervention
Back blows and thrusts are for ineffective cough or unconscious patients
Plain X-ray can be normal in radiolucent foreign bodies
Bronchodilators and corticosteroids do not treat foreign body aspiration
explanation.pearls: Asymmetric breath sounds, persistent unilateral wheeze or new-onset chronic cough in a toddler with a witnessed choking event are red flags for retained foreign body even with a normal radiograph; bronchoscopy under anaesthetic is both diagnostic and therapeutic.

subtopic_detail: Faltering growth in an exclusively breastfed infant
stem: You are the general practice registrar reviewing a 5-month-old exclusively breastfed non-Aboriginal infant whose weight has dropped from the 50th centile at birth to below the 3rd centile today, crossing two major centile lines over 8 weeks. His mother describes good attachment with feeds approximately every 3 hours but reports that he has slow weight gain at recent maternal child health nurse visits. There are no concerning features in the history and developmental milestones are appropriate for age.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 °C, HR 132, RR 36, SpO2 99% room air, BP 86/52 mmHg, weight 5.2 kg
data_table.Examination: Alert and engaged, normal tone and posture, soft anterior fontanelle, no dysmorphic features, normal cardiac and respiratory examination, abdomen soft non-tender with no organomegaly
data_table.Investigations: Growth chart shows downward crossing of 2 centile lines over 8 weeks, no bloods or imaging performed to date

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Arrange admission for nasogastric supplemental feeds while a paediatric metabolic workup including amino acids, acylcarnitine profile and very long chain fatty acids is performed and reviewed by metabolic specialists
options.rationale: Inpatient metabolic workup is reserved for atypical features or no response to standard feeding interventions; first-line management focuses on feeding optimisation and a stepped outpatient workup. (Source: RACGP Red Book Faltering Growth)

options.text: Arrange a structured feeding history and observed feed with lactation support, a stool history for malabsorption, a focused examination and baseline investigations including FBC iron studies UEC LFT TFT urinalysis and coeliac serology
options.rationale: Australian guidance recommends a structured feeding assessment, observed feed and baseline screen (FBC, iron, UEC, LFT, TFT, urinalysis, coeliac) before escalating to inpatient or specialist workup in non-organic faltering growth. (Source: RACGP Red Book Faltering Growth)

options.text: Refer immediately to a paediatric gastroenterologist for an upper gastrointestinal endoscopy, jejunal biopsy and stool elastase to evaluate for malabsorption due to coeliac disease, cystic fibrosis or eosinophilic enteropathy
options.rationale: Endoscopy and stool elastase are second-line tests after a baseline screen and feeding assessment have failed to identify a cause. (Source: RCH CPG: Faltering growth)

options.text: Recommend immediate introduction of cow's milk formula every 3 hours alongside continued breastfeeding and review weight gain in 2 weeks regardless of the maternal preference for exclusive breastfeeding
options.rationale: Formula top-ups are introduced only after a structured feeding assessment confirms inadequate breastmilk transfer; routine introduction overrides maternal preference and undermines lactation. (Source: NHMRC Infant Feeding Guidelines)

options.text: Reassure the mother that the centile chart drop reflects genetic potential and adequate exclusive breastfeeding and arrange a routine weight review at the maternal child health nurse appointment in 4 weeks' time
options.rationale: Crossing 2 or more centile lines is faltering growth and warrants structured assessment; reassurance without further evaluation misses treatable causes. (Source: RACGP Red Book Faltering Growth)

explanation.summary: Crossing 2 or more major centile lines in infancy meets the definition of faltering growth and warrants stepped assessment in primary care. The first step is a structured feeding history, observed feed with lactation support, a comprehensive examination and a baseline investigation panel. Inpatient or specialist evaluation is reserved for severe deficit, red flags or failure to respond. Reassurance without assessment risks missing breastfeeding difficulties, coeliac disease, cystic fibrosis or other treatable causes.
key_points: Crossing 2 major centiles defines faltering growth
First-line is feeding assessment and outpatient baseline screen
Baseline screen: FBC, iron, UEC, LFT, TFT, urinalysis, coeliac
Lactation support precedes formula top-up in breastfeeding infants
Specialist referral is for red flags or failed first-line management
explanation.pearls: Red flags that bypass the staged outpatient workup and trigger admission include weight under 70% of expected, severe undernutrition signs, suspected child protection concerns, dysmorphic features or atypical examination findings.

subtopic_detail: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes at 34 weeks
stem: You are the obstetric registrar at a tertiary maternity hospital reviewing a 34+0 week gestation primigravida who presents with a 6-hour history of clear fluid loss per vaginum. Sterile speculum confirms pooling of liquor with positive nitrazine test and ultrasound shows reduced amniotic fluid index. She is afebrile with no uterine activity and the fetal heart rate trace is reassuring. Her group B streptococcus swab from 36 weeks is not yet available because of her current gestation.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 °C, HR 78, RR 16, SpO2 99% room air, BP 118/72 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender uterus, no palpable contractions, cephalic presentation, fundal height 33 cm, no per-vaginal blood loss
data_table.Investigations: Speculum confirms pooling, nitrazine positive, ultrasound AFI 4 cm, fetal heart rate trace category 1, FBC and CRP within reference ranges, group B streptococcus swab not available, cervical length not measured

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Discharge home with twice weekly review and instructions to return if fever vaginal bleeding decreased fetal movements or rupture of fresh liquor, with planned induction of labour booked for 37+0 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Inpatient surveillance and empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics are standard after PPROM under 37 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM)

options.text: Proceed immediately to lower segment caesarean section under spinal anaesthesia given the gestation and uncertain group B streptococcus status with a single dose of intravenous cefazolin 2 g at induction of anaesthesia
options.rationale: Cephalic presentation with reassuring trace and no labour or chorioamnionitis is not an indication for immediate caesarean at 34 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM)

options.text: Admit for tocolysis with nifedipine, intramuscular betamethasone 11.4 mg now and in 24 hours, intravenous magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection and reassess in 48 hours before discussing planned delivery
options.rationale: At 34 weeks magnesium sulfate neuroprotection is not indicated (used under 30-32 weeks) and tocolysis is not used in PPROM after 34 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroids)

options.text: Admit for inpatient expectant management with intravenous benzylpenicillin and oral erythromycin, 4-hourly observations, intermittent CTG and plan induction of labour by 37+0 weeks if not delivered earlier
options.rationale: PPROM at 34 weeks is managed by admission, latency antibiotics (benzylpenicillin plus erythromycin), observation for chorioamnionitis and planned delivery by 37 weeks if conservative management continues. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM)

options.text: Admit for immediate induction of labour with vaginal prostaglandin gel and intravenous oxytocin titration and intrapartum intravenous benzylpenicillin for group B streptococcus prophylaxis throughout the labour
options.rationale: Routine immediate induction at 34 weeks does not improve outcomes versus expectant management to 37 weeks in uncomplicated PPROM; trial evidence (PPROMT) favours expectant management. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM)

explanation.summary: PPROM at 34 to 36+6 weeks is managed by admission, latency antibiotics with intravenous benzylpenicillin plus oral erythromycin and observation for signs of chorioamnionitis. The PPROMT trial supports planned expectant management to 37 weeks unless there is infection, abruption or non-reassuring fetal status. Antenatal corticosteroids are not given after 34+6 weeks. Magnesium sulfate neuroprotection is used in threatened preterm birth under 30-32 weeks, not at 34 weeks.
key_points: PPROM 34-36 weeks: admit, latency antibiotics, observe for chorioamnionitis
Antibiotics: benzylpenicillin plus erythromycin
Planned delivery by 37 weeks if no infection or compromise
Antenatal steroids beyond 34+6 weeks are generally not indicated
Magnesium neuroprotection is for preterm birth under 30-32 weeks
explanation.pearls: PPROMT (Lancet 2016) showed planned expectant management at 34-36 weeks reduced neonatal respiratory distress without increasing chorioamnionitis or sepsis compared with immediate birth; intrapartum group B strep cover is added if swab is positive or unknown.

subtopic_detail: Gestational diabetes mellitus diagnosis at 28 weeks
stem: You are the antenatal clinic registrar reviewing a 31-year-old G2P1 woman at 28 weeks' gestation. Her routine 75 g 2-hour oral glucose tolerance test results are back. She has no symptoms, has gained 11 kg this pregnancy and her body mass index at booking was 31 kg/m2. She had no GDM in her first pregnancy, which ended in a normal vaginal birth of a 3.5 kg infant. Her fetus is appropriately grown on today's growth scan with normal amniotic fluid.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 °C, HR 76, RR 14, SpO2 99% room air, BP 116/74 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Fundal height 28 cm, cephalic presentation, fetal heart 142/min, no oedema
data_table.Investigations: 75 g OGTT fasting 5.4 mmol/L, 1 hour 11.2 mmol/L, 2 hour 8.0 mmol/L; HbA1c 5.4%, urinalysis no glucose no ketones, growth scan within reference ranges, amniotic fluid index 14 cm

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Diagnose gestational diabetes mellitus, refer to the diabetes in pregnancy multidisciplinary clinic, commence self-monitoring of capillary blood glucose four times daily and start individualised lifestyle and dietary modification
options.rationale: ADIPS 75 g OGTT diagnostic thresholds (fasting 5.1, 1 hour 10.0, 2 hour 8.5 mmol/L) are met (1 hour 11.2) confirming GDM; first-line management is the diabetes in pregnancy clinic with self-monitoring and lifestyle intervention. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines GDM)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and Treatment of GDM

options.text: Repeat the 75 g oral glucose tolerance test in 2 weeks because the 2 hour value is below the diagnostic threshold and the fasting glucose was within the reference range at this current testing point
options.rationale: Only one OGTT value needs to exceed the ADIPS threshold to confirm GDM; the 1 hour value of 11.2 meets the criterion. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines GDM)

options.text: Reassure that the results are within normal range for the third trimester and recommend a follow-up HbA1c at 6 weeks postnatal with no further antenatal glucose testing or dietary intervention required at this stage
options.rationale: OGTT values, not HbA1c, define GDM in pregnancy and the 1 hour value here is diagnostic; reassurance misses the diagnosis. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines GDM)

options.text: Commence metformin 500 mg twice daily and refer for endocrinology review in 4 weeks while continuing routine antenatal care and reserving any insulin therapy until after delivery if hyperglycaemia persists postpartum
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy follows a trial of dietary and lifestyle intervention with home glucose monitoring; immediate metformin without monitoring is not first-line. (Source: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and Treatment of GDM)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and Treatment of GDM

options.text: Arrange a 100 g 3-hour oral glucose tolerance test to confirm gestational diabetes mellitus using two abnormal values out of four glucose readings as per Carpenter-Coustan diagnostic thresholds for confirmation
options.rationale: Australia uses the 75 g 2-hour OGTT with ADIPS thresholds; the 100 g 3-hour Carpenter-Coustan test is not the diagnostic standard here. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines GDM)

explanation.summary: The Australian ADIPS diagnostic 75 g 2 hour OGTT thresholds for gestational diabetes are fasting 5.1 mmol/L, 1 hour 10.0 mmol/L and 2 hour 8.5 mmol/L. Any single abnormal value confirms the diagnosis. First-line management is referral to a diabetes in pregnancy clinic, home capillary glucose monitoring four times daily, individualised lifestyle and dietary advice. Metformin or insulin is added if targets are not met after a 1 to 2 week trial. HbA1c is not used to diagnose GDM in pregnancy.
key_points: ADIPS thresholds: fasting 5.1, 1 hour 10.0, 2 hour 8.5 mmol/L
Any one abnormal OGTT value confirms GDM
First-line: diabetes in pregnancy clinic, SMBG, lifestyle
Add metformin or insulin if targets not met after 1-2 weeks
Postpartum 75 g OGTT at 6-12 weeks to reclassify
explanation.pearls: Women with GDM have a 50% lifetime risk of type 2 diabetes; arrange a 75 g 2 hour OGTT at 6 to 12 weeks postpartum and an annual fasting glucose or HbA1c thereafter, plus encourage breastfeeding and weight optimisation.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and Treatment of GDM
